perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall, at Disclosing Party's request, return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information and all other tangible materials and devices provided to the Receiving Party as Confidential Information, or at Disclosing Party's option, certify destruction of the same.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party will manage confidential information according to its internal policies.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates the return or destruction of confidential information at the Disclosing Party's request. The change allows the Receiving Party to manage it according to its internal policies. This contradicts the Disclosing Party's right to request the return of information and creates uncertainty about whether the Receiving Party's internal policies align with protecting the Disclosing Party's confidential information, which is contradicting with the clause in Section 3 where states that 'The parties acknowledge that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties acknowledge that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies, to such injunctive or equitable relief as may be deemed proper by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The original clause gave the Disclosing Party the right to request return/destruction of confidential information. The changed text now allows the Receiving Party to manage confidential information according to its internal policies, removing the Disclosing Party's control and contradicting their right to request the return of confidential information. This undermines the purpose of the contradicted text, which acknowledges monetary damages may not be sufficient and that the Disclosing Party is entitled to injunctive relief, since the change now weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to protect confidential information in the first place.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree to comply with all applicable international and national laws that apply to (i) any Confidential Information, or (ii) any product (or any part thereof), process or service that is the direct product of the Confidential Information, including the U.S. Export Administration Regulations, as well as end-user, end-use and destination restrictions issued by U.S. and other governments. For additional information on exporting Microsoft products, see http://www.microsoft.com/exporting/.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties will make reasonable efforts to comply with relevant laws.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause imposes a strict obligation to comply with all applicable laws. The change weakens this to "reasonable efforts," creating a loophole for non-compliance. This could directly contradict Section 3, where is referring to monetary damages that 'may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties acknowledge that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies, to such injunctive or equitable relief as may be deemed proper by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated full compliance with applicable laws, whereas the changed text only requires 'reasonable efforts'. This directly contradicts the Contradicted Text, which states that monetary damages may not be sufficient for unauthorized disclosure of confidential information, and seeks injunctive relief. If the parties are only making 'reasonable efforts' to comply with laws protecting Confidential Information, this undermines the basis for seeking injunctive relief as a remedy, because it suggests that breaches may occur even with reasonable efforts. The original text ensured full compliance, whereas the Changed Text introduces a loophole for non-compliance, directly impacting the enforceability and remedies outlined in the Contradicted Text.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be binding upon and inure to the benefit of each party's respective successors and lawful assigns; provided, however, that neither party may assign this Agreement (whether by operation of law, sale of securities or assets, merger or otherwise), in whole or in part, without the prior written approval of the other party. Any attempted assignment in violation of this Section shall be void.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement can be assigned by either party with written notice to the other.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause explicitly prohibits assignment without prior written approval. The change allows assignment with only written notice, contradicting the need for approval. The initial clause also stated 'Any attempted assignment in violation of this Section shall be void', which is now contradicted.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (g) This Agreement shall be binding upon and inure to the benefit of each party's respective successors and lawful assigns; provided, however, that neither party may assign this Agreement (whether by operation of law, sale of securities or assets, merger or otherwise), in whole or in part, without the prior written approval of the other party. Any attempted assignment in violation of this Section shall be void.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly prohibits assignment without prior written approval, stating 'Any attempted assignment in violation of this Section shall be void'. The changed text allows assignment with written notice only, which directly contradicts the need for approval stipulated in both the original text and the contradicted text. This change introduces a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: If either Disclosing Party or the Receiving Party employs attorneys to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys' fees and costs.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party will bear its own attorneys' fees and costs.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause allows the prevailing party to recover attorneys' fees. The change stipulates that each party bears its own costs, contradicting the original clause. This is also contradictory to Section 3, where it states that 'monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties acknowledge that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies, to such injunctive or equitable relief as may be deemed proper by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The changed text states that each party will bear its own attorneys' fees and costs, directly contradicting the original clause, which allows the prevailing party to recover attorneys' fees. This also contradicts the contradicted text because if monetary damages are not sufficient, the ability to recover attorney fees would be necessary for injunctive relief, which is now removed by the changed text. This aligns with the Omissions - In Text Contradiction definition, as essential language regarding attorney fees has been removed, creating a conflict with the contradicted section.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement with or without cause upon ninety (90) days prior written notice to the other party. All sections of this Agreement relating to the rights and obligations of the parties concerning Confidential Information disclosed during the term of the Agreement shall survive any such termination.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement with 30 days' notice. Upon termination, all obligations cease.
perturbation.explanation: The original termination clause requires 90 days' notice and specifies that confidentiality obligations survive termination. The change reduces the notice period to 30 days and states that all obligations cease upon termination, contradicting the survival of confidentiality clauses. This is directly in conflict with clause 2(a), which states that Receiving Party should 'Refrain from disclosing any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to third parties for two (2) years following the date that Disclosing Party first discloses such Confidential Information to Receiving Party'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Receiving Party shall: (i) Refrain from disclosing any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to third parties for two (2) years following the date that Disclosing Party first discloses such Confidential Information to Receiving Party, except as expressly provided in Sections 2(b) and 2(c) of this Agreement; (ii) Take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential information, but no less than reasonable care, to keep confidential the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party; (iii) Refrain from disclosing, reproducing, summarizing and/or distributing Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party except in pursuance of Receiving Party's business relationship with Disclosing Party, and only as otherwise provided hereunder; and (iv) Refrain from reverse engineering, decompiling or disassembling any software code and/or pre-release hardware devices disclosed by Disclosing Party to Receiving Party under the terms of this Agreement, except as expressly permitted by applicable law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original termination clause, reducing the notice period from 90 to 30 days and stating that all obligations cease upon termination. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which specifies that the Receiving Party must refrain from disclosing Confidential Information for two years after disclosure. Because the changed text states that all obligations cease, it creates a direct conflict with the confidentiality obligations outlined in the contradicted text. This aligns with the Omissions - In Text Contradiction definition.
